title: Chapter 7, Verse 12
shlok:
ये चैव सात्त्विका भावा राजसास्तामसाश्च ये ।
मत्त एवेति तान्विद्धि न त्वहं तेषु ते मयि ॥
transliteration:
ye caiva sāttvikā bhāvā rājasās tāmasāś ca ye |
matta eveti tān viddhi na tv ahaṁ teṣu te mayi ||
translation: Whatever states of being exist — sattvic, rajasic, or tamasic — know that they all arise from Me alone. Yet I am not in them; they are in Me.
interpretation: In Chapter 7, Krishna is explaining His nature and relationship to the manifest world. Here He addresses the three gunas — the fundamental qualities that color all of existence. Sattva is the quality of clarity, harmony, and purity. Rajas is the quality of passion, desire, and restless activity. Tamas is the quality of inertia, heaviness, and dullness. Every state of mind, every mood, every condition in nature is some mixture of these three. Krishna says all of it arises from Him, yet He is not bound by any of it. The asymmetry is important: the gunas live within Him, but He does not live within them. He is their source without being their captive.
application: Most of Sid's inner struggles — the restless anxiety about whether the PM path is moving fast enough, the heavy inertia that makes it easy to defer financial discipline, the sharp clarity that shows up when he is fully locked in on a problem — are expressions of the three gunas cycling through him. The verse offers a practical reframe: these states arise in you, but you are not them. Observing which guna is running the show at any given moment, without collapsing into it, is the beginning of the kind of discernment a good PM needs as much as a spiritual practitioner does.
question: When rajas or tamas is clearly running your day, can you name it plainly and work with it rather than pretending the state is the whole truth of who you are?
